I like it, I like it, I'm going to come here to eat someday *laughs* *laughs* *trn laughs* but there are things I don't let slide, and she knows it. I might make a mistake because I've lost almost all my memory. The other day, I left my shoes there, and she scolded me, "Dad, put your shoes away." She understands that it's not intentional, of course. And you can tell the memory problems are noticeable when you can't remember someone's name or a word doesn't come to you. No, what happens is that I have this flaw, not a flaw, but I leave something and then I have to think about where I put it because if you get nervous, it's worse. Uh-hum, then your mind gets in such a state that you don't even know where you left it. So I think, well, if it's not there, it must be here, and if not, in the bed or the bathroom, the watch or the glasses. Many times she says, "Dad, your glasses are there." The other day, I was carrying my wallet and kept looking for it. *looks at trn* What was it, girl? What was it? I don't remember what it was. I don't know what it could be. I don't remember either, and my son said, "But Dad, you have it." He came to see me and said, "Dad, look where your wallet is, under the bench." The wallet was there. I had been going around the house. It's not that small, not big either, but that's fine. Uh-hum, damn, if it's not here, where could it be? Then I went to the bedroom because I used to sleep in that room, my room. But of course, since they are married now, they want a bit more space, to be more comfortable, and I don't mind where I sleep. Sometimes I go into the room because I've been sleeping in that room for more than thirty years. *looks at trn* Thirty years I've been sleeping there, of course, but as I said, I remember things from fifty years ago more than suddenly now. Yes, sir, because I had an accident more than twenty years ago, and I have a brother who, forty-two years ago, minus two months, had my son. So I remember that. I remember, I remember a lot, I remember when I was little, I remember the accidents I've had, a donkey that bit me too. She might not do it with bad intentions; it's just her temperament, and then she's a sweetheart, very clean and hardworking like no one else. I say this here and anywhere else. She has always been like that, while her brother has been the opposite, more laid-back and calm. But she, right now, goes to work, goes to bed with her husband, and you don't have to worry because he doesn't have to call her. However, maybe she does have to call him, just like what happened to me with my wife, may she rest in peace, the poor thing. But I never had to call her; she was very responsible with her work. Yes, of course, then, of course, the circumstances of life change, and you adapt, and the jobs too. I've been very lucky with jobs, well, not today because there are no jobs, nothing at all. I got married well, but poor, but very well, you know. I took care of my people, my things, and then I brought some money of my own after getting married. I brought fifty thousand pesetas after getting married, forty-five thousand pesetas for the furniture, and another thirty-five thousand that the bride brought. A little bit of money that we both had. Then we got married, and I was paying for a small house that was forty meters, it wasn't much, just a small area with four, six, eight, ten, or twenty houses. You don't remember that, of course not. I paid two hundred and fifty pesetas a month, and I was working with my brothers in Gandía. Then my sister-in-law saw these apartments here on the lower street because her aunt and my wife worked together in the same factory. Later, she had hip problems and couldn't climb the stairs anymore, so we moved here. "To hell with it, and then of course they fired everyone. The factory had been in ruins for at least four or five years or more, with all the machinery. I had to pay a hundred and something thousand pesetas for the apartment installment. So, of course, my wife was worried that they were going to take the apartment away from us. She started to get on my case, and I didn't argue with her. Sometimes I even cried, but she doesn't remember any of that because she was very young. Do you remember any of that? No, not at all. Maybe your brother does. I said they won't take the apartment away from us. Then, how are we going to pay for it? My parents couldn't lend us less than five hundred thousand. Look, go to your brother Ángel. I have two brothers, one named Ángel and the other Juanjo. Or if not, go to Juanjo. I said, okay, don't worry. The following week, we went to see the grandparents. I can still picture it. I felt very embarrassed to ask them for six hundred thousand pesetas that I needed for the apartment. So, what do you want? Come on, just tell me. I said, look, lend me six hundred thousand pesetas because I can't pay it. I could pay it, but I had to take her along, and she was unemployed, but she was getting a pittance from unemployment benefits. Well, maybe she was getting, I can't tell you the exact amount, but very little. So, I went there with her and said, come on, let's do this. He said, look, take your bankbook and send it to Benimamet, and I'll send my bankbook to your bank in Nazaret. And indeed, within a month, I had the six hundred thousand pesetas in the bank with my bankbook from there, and I made the transfer with her bankbook here."
